course_info.title: Comparative Government and Politics
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 1 hour per session

class_schedule.topic: Why Compare? Methods in Comparative Politics
learning_objectives: Explain the purpose of comparative political analysis
Distinguish between most-similar and most-different research designs
Identify key variables: regime type, institutions, political culture
readings: Kesselman et al., Introduction to Comparative Politics Ch. 1
Lijphart, 'Comparative Politics and the Comparative Method'

lecture_notes.introduction: Comparative politics examines political systems across countries to identify patterns, test theories, and understand variation. It is the laboratory of political science.
key_concepts: Comparative method as a quasi-experimental approach
Most-similar systems design (Mill's Method of Difference)
Most-different systems design (Mill's Method of Agreement)
Key variables: institutions, culture, economy, history
Case selection and selection bias
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a comparative study: selecting cases to test whether electoral systems affect party fragmentation
setup_requirements: Country data sheets for case selection

review.value: Comparing countries that are alike in most respects but differ in the outcome of interest, to isolate the causal variable
The distortion that occurs when cases are chosen non-randomly, such as studying only democracies to explain democratization

learning_objectives: Define democracy, authoritarianism, and hybrid regimes
Apply Polity and Freedom House indicators
Analyze the global trend of democratic backsliding
readings: Freedom House, Freedom in the World Report (latest summary)

lecture_notes.introduction: The most fundamental distinction in comparative politics is between democratic and authoritarian rule. But the boundary is increasingly blurred.
key_concepts: Procedural versus substantive definitions of democracy
Types of authoritarianism: military, single-party, personalist, theocratic
Hybrid regimes and competitive authoritarianism
Measuring democracy: Polity IV, Freedom House, V-Dem
Democratic backsliding and autocratization
lecture_notes.demonstration: Classifying 10 countries on a democracy-authoritarianism spectrum using Freedom House data

review.value: A regime that holds elections and permits opposition but systematically tilts the playing field through media control, harassment, and institutional manipulation
The gradual erosion of democratic norms and institutions from within, often by elected leaders who undermine checks and balances

learning_objectives: Compare presidential, parliamentary, and semi-presidential systems
Analyze how executive-legislative relations shape policy outcomes
Evaluate the strengths and weaknesses of each system type

lecture_notes.introduction: How governments are structured fundamentally shapes how they govern. The choice between presidential and parliamentary systems has profound consequences.
key_concepts: Presidential systems: separation of powers, fixed terms (US, Brazil)
Parliamentary systems: fusion of powers, confidence mechanism (UK, Germany)
Semi-presidential systems: dual executive (France, Russia)
Legislative organization: unicameral vs. bicameral
Linz's critique: why presidentialism is risky for new democracies
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparative table exercise: mapping executive-legislative relations in the US, UK, France, and Japan

review.value: The executive (prime minister) is drawn from and accountable to the legislature; government falls if it loses legislative confidence
Presidential systems create dual democratic legitimacy (president vs. legislature), fixed terms prevent flexible crisis resolution, and winner-take-all dynamics intensify conflict

learning_objectives: Compare majoritarian, proportional, and mixed electoral systems
Apply Duverger's Law to predict party system effects
Analyze how electoral rules shape representation and governance

lecture_notes.introduction: Electoral systems are the rules that translate votes into seats. Different rules produce radically different outcomes for representation and governance.
key_concepts: First-past-the-post (FPTP) and single-member districts
Proportional representation (PR): list PR and STV
Mixed systems: MMP (Germany, New Zealand)
Duverger's Law: FPTP tends toward two parties
Party systems: dominant, two-party, multiparty, fragmented
lecture_notes.demonstration: Simulation: running the same election results through FPTP and PR systems to compare seat allocations

review.value: The observation that single-member district plurality systems tend to produce two-party systems, while proportional representation tends to produce multiparty systems
Electoral systems that allocate seats in proportion to votes received, typically using party lists or transferable votes in multi-member districts

class_schedule.topic: Political Culture, Civil Society, and Social Movements
learning_objectives: Define political culture and assess its role in shaping regime outcomes
Analyze the relationship between civil society and democracy
Evaluate social movements as agents of political change

lecture_notes.introduction: Institutions alone do not determine political outcomes. The values, beliefs, and associational life of citizens are equally important.
key_concepts: Political culture: Almond and Verba's civic culture typology
Social capital and its role in democratic governance
Civil society: NGOs, media, religious organizations, labor unions
Social movements: resource mobilization and political opportunity structure
Digital activism and new forms of political participation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparative case study: civil society in Scandinavia versus Russia

review.value: Networks of trust, norms of reciprocity, and civic engagement that facilitate collective action and democratic governance (Putnam)
The external conditions that encourage or discourage social movement activity, including political openings, elite divisions, and available allies

class_schedule.topic: Comparative Political Economy and the Welfare State
learning_objectives: Compare varieties of capitalism: liberal, coordinated, and state-led
Analyze the three worlds of welfare capitalism
Evaluate how political institutions shape economic policy
readings: Esping-Andersen, Three Worlds of Welfare Capitalism Ch. 1 (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: How do different political systems organize their economies? Comparative political economy reveals that capitalism comes in many varieties.
key_concepts: Varieties of capitalism: liberal market economies vs. coordinated market economies
State-led development: East Asian developmental states
Three welfare state regimes: liberal, conservative-corporatist, social-democratic
Globalization's impact on domestic economic policy
Inequality and redistribution across regime types
lecture_notes.demonstration: Data comparison: social spending, inequality, and economic performance across the US, Germany, Sweden, and South Korea

review.value: Liberal market economies (US, UK) rely on market coordination; coordinated market economies (Germany, Japan) rely on institutional coordination among firms, unions, and the state
Liberal (means-tested, US/UK), conservative-corporatist (status-preserving, Germany/France), social-democratic (universal, Scandinavia)

questions.question: Explain the most-similar systems design in comparative politics. Why is it useful, and what is its main limitation?
questions.answer: Most-similar systems design compares countries that share many characteristics but differ in the outcome being studied, allowing the researcher to isolate the variable that explains the difference. It is useful because it controls for many potential confounding variables naturally. Its main limitation is that perfectly matched cases are rare, and unmeasured differences between seemingly similar countries may actually explain the outcome.

questions.question: What is competitive authoritarianism and how does it differ from a full democracy and a closed authoritarian regime?
questions.answer: Competitive authoritarianism is a hybrid regime where elections occur and opposition is allowed to compete, but the playing field is systematically tilted in favor of the incumbent through media control, selective prosecution of opponents, manipulation of electoral rules, and abuse of state resources. Unlike a full democracy, meaningful competition is undermined. Unlike closed authoritarianism, opposition has real (if constrained) space to organize and occasionally win. Examples include Russia under Putin and Venezuela under Chavez/Maduro.

quizzes.topic: Institutions, Electoral Systems, and Political Culture

questions.question: State Duverger's Law and explain the mechanical and psychological effects that produce the predicted outcome.
questions.answer: Duverger's Law states that single-member district plurality (FPTP) electoral systems tend to produce two-party systems. The mechanical effect is that FPTP systematically under-represents smaller parties whose votes are spread across districts without winning pluralities. The psychological effect is that voters, anticipating this waste, strategically abandon third parties to vote for the lesser of two evils, and donors and activists similarly concentrate resources on viable candidates.

questions.question: According to Putnam, what is social capital and why does it matter for democratic governance? Provide one example of how declining social capital could weaken democracy.
questions.answer: Social capital refers to networks of trust, norms of reciprocity, and habits of civic engagement that enable collective action. Putnam argues it matters for democracy because it facilitates cooperation, holds officials accountable, and builds the interpersonal trust needed for democratic institutions to function. An example of its decline weakening democracy: as civic organizations (bowling leagues, PTAs, unions) decline, citizens become more isolated, less informed, and less able to organize collectively, making them more vulnerable to populist manipulation and less capable of holding government accountable.

major_assignments.title: Comparative Political Systems Analysis
major_assignments.description: Select two countries with different political systems and conduct a structured comparative analysis examining institutions, political culture, and policy outcomes. Apply comparative methods learned in class.
objectives: Apply most-similar or most-different systems design
Evaluate how political culture interacts with institutions
Draw theoretically informed conclusions from comparison
deliverables: Structured comparison table of key institutional features
Annotated bibliography with at least 10 sources

criteria: Appropriate comparative design chosen and justified
Accurate description of both political systems
Analysis of how institutions shape outcomes
Appropriate use of comparative politics concepts
Deep institutional analysis of both countries
Theoretically informed argument with strong evidence

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes assessing understanding of comparative concepts and country knowledge

participation.description: Engagement in discussions, simulations, and case study exercises

major_assignments.description: Comparative political systems analysis paper and presentation

midterm_examination.description: Exam covering comparative methods, regime types, and institutions

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam requiring comparative analysis across multiple countries

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance is required. More than 3 unexcused absences will lower the participation grade to zero.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late papers are penalized 5% per day. No extensions granted within 24 hours of deadline.
course_policies.collaboration: Study groups are encouraged. All written work must be individually authored. Shared data collection is permitted with disclosure.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original. Plagiarism results in course failure. AI tools may assist with research but may not generate submitted text.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to Freedom House, Polity, and V-Dem datasets; university library databases; course LMS.
